summary: Simon Späti argues that Vim is not just an editor but a powerful language for text manipulation that every programmer should learn. He explains the grammar behind Vim - verbs, subjects, objects, and motions - which allows users to combine a few memorized commands into hundreds of combinations. After years of hitting productivity limits with standard editors, he discovered that Vim's modal editing approach optimizes for editing rather than writing from scratch. While acknowledging the steep learning curve, he shares how Vim transformed not just his coding but his entire workflow, from writing to browsing, making him a better developer through deeper terminal fluency.
key_points: Vim's power comes from its grammar-like language (verbs, subjects, objects) that lets you combine commands rather than memorize thousands of shortcuts
Vim motions are universal and available in other editors, browsers, and applications - you can learn them anywhere
Vim is the only editor that optimizes for editing existing text rather than writing from a blank page, using modal editing (normal, insert, visual, command modes)
The learning curve is steep but pays off - Vim is the only editor where you get faster with time
Learning Vim naturally improves terminal and Linux skills, leading to broader developer competency
money_quotes: I've since learned that Vim is the only editor that you get faster using with time.
Everyone who types on a computer eight hours a day should learn the Vim language.
Vim is the only editor that optimizes editing text instead of writing from a blank page.
With a couple of verbs and objects, you can already know hundreds of combinations without memorizing each one individually.
It felt liberating, moving my cursor with the precision of a surgeon.
An underrated skill in general among programmers is using the terminal.
Vim changed not only my workflow but how I was able to edit at the speed of thought.
Working in Neovim is one of the highlights of my everyday work.
key_insight: Vim's grammar-based approach to text editing - combining verbs, motions, and objects - transforms memorizing shortcuts into learning a composable language that makes you faster over time rather than hitting a productivity ceiling.
